0: <title>Social life cycle assessment of average Irish dairy farm </title>
Received: 11 June 2016 / Accepted: 9 December 2016/Published online: 24 December 2016 Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 2016
1: Purpose The majority of sustainability studies of dairy farms focused on environmental performance and profitability; how- ever, social aspect has been hardly assessed. This study aims to investigate the social impacts of dairy farm via a case study using a social life cycle assessment framework.

Methods The assessment was carried out applying the social LCA Guideline by UNEP-SETAC. Nineteen suitable social indicators were selected from four stakeholder categories of the guideline. Characterization and normalization were further developed based on data availability. National farm survey data was used as foreground data for farm activities, supple- mented with background data from public database and life cycle working environment (LCWE) data by Gabi database. All indicators were divided into three groups: functional unit- related quantitative indicators, non-functional unit-related quantitative indicators and semi-quantitative indicators. Results and discussion Irish dairy farming has positive social impacts on value chain actors and society, predominantly pos- itive impacts for local community and generally positive values for workers. The main negative impacts are health and safety issue, equal opportunity for workers, and safe and healthy living conditions for the local community. Possible actions to improve the social performance include introducing more efficient and robotic milk production systems; applying

better handling methods and using real time decision support to operational management for emissions reduction. Conclusions This study is the first attempt of social LCA in Ireland. It demonstrated a possible method to carry out SLCA for Irish dairy sector. The results identified the positive and negative social hotspot of dairy farm with recommendation for future improvement.
Keywords Milk production . Social hotspot . Social life cycle assessment Sustainability

4: It is now well recognized that sustainability assessment needs to consider three interacting "pillars": environment, economy, and society (Giddings et al. 2002). In the context of life cycle thinking and the life cycle assessment framework, methods have been developed for the environmental pillar (Environmental Life Cycle Assessment, ELCA) (Finnveden et al. 2009) and the economic pillar (Life Cycle Costing, LCC) (Hoogmartens et al. 2014), but the social pillar is less well developed (Fan et al. 2015).

Social Life Cycle Assessment (SLCA) was derived from social impact assessment (Freudenburg 1986) and followed the structure of ELCA (i) goal and scope definition, (ii) inven- tory analysis, (iii) impact assessment, and (iv) interpretation (ISO 2006a). However, SLCA is quite different in practice. Most of the social impacts in SLCA do not depend on, and are not necessarily proportional to, physical flows but reflect the influence of company behavior with respect to stakeholders (Jorgensen et al. 2008). This leads to the problem of linking impacts to a functional unit, which has been overcome to some extent by combining quantitative and semi-quantitative social indicators for SLCA (Manik et al. 2013; Martínez-
1 UCD School of Biosystems and Food Engineering, Agriculture and Food Science Centre, University College Dublin, Belfield, Dublin, Ireland
Blanco et al. 2014; Dong and Ng 2015) or using the new reference unit "reporting organization" from organization LCA (Martinez-Blanco et al. 2015).

SLCA also demands location-specific data for modeling the system because of the significant cultural and economic differences between countries (Benoît Norris 2013). Suitable high spatial and temporal resolution data are scarce and are not found in many LCA databases (Finnveden et al. 2009), and collecting such data can be quite difficult (Benoît Norris 2013). For example, it is very difficult to collect data like child labor at a company level. So, such data are generally only available at sectoral or national scale (e.g., International Labor Organization (ILO) database) (ILO 2012). In addition, many social impact data are normally expressed in terms of the extent to which best practice is implemented (Dong and Ng 2015). This means building database for SLCA is still at its infancy. The Social Hotspot Database (SHDB) is the most widely used social impact database for SLCA (Ekener- Petersen and Finnveden 2013). However, some indicators in SHDB are based on extrapolation and more sectoral data need to be collected in the future (Martínez-Blanco et al. 2014). In Ireland, the database of the Central Statistics Office is the major source of agricultural data. However, during the aggre- gation of data, some detailed information cannot be derived from this input-output database (Yan et al. 2013a).

In terms of methodology, as the development of SLCA is still at its early stage, there is no standard method for SLCA (Hunkeler 2006; Norris 2006; Weidema 2006; Jorgensen et al. 2008; Dreyer et al. 2010). All of these factors limited SCLA application. To facilitate the SLCA application, recent re- searches focused on solving issues from epistemology to prac- tical application in local level. Instead of discussing methodo- logical issues in diverse SLCA approaches, Iofrida et al. (2016) used the identified research paradigms to justify the diversity of SLCA methodology and suggested an urgent reinforcement of the theoretical bases of SLCA, with focus on epistemological discourse. Zamagni et al. (2015) analyzed different SLCA methodologies and discussed the fundamental differences between ELCA and SLCA. Weidema (2016) proposed a streamlined impacts assessment to reduce data requirement and detailed impact pathway description in practical SLCA and De Luca et al. (2015) adopted a mixed method of qualita- tive technique and multicriteria analysis to identify locally rel- evant social characteristics in agricultural systems.

Currently, more than 70% of SLCA studies (Iofrida et al. 2016) follow the "SLCA Guidelines of the Life Cycle Initiative" of the United Nations Environment Programme and Society of Environmental Toxicology and Chemistry (UNEP-SETAC) (UNEP-SETAC 2009; Benoît-Norris et al. 2011). The "SLCA Guidelines" provide a general framework for conducting a study and classify 31 social issues into five stakeholder categories including "worker," "consumer," "local community," "society," and "value chain factor"

(UNEP-SETAC 2009). Depending on the scope of the study and availability of data, a SLCA should include some or all of the listed social impacts from the "Guidelines" (Benoît et al. 2010). Benoit and her colleagues provide prototypes for conducting SLCA for orange juice and strawberry yogurt (Benoit et al. 2011a, b). Ekener-Petersen and Finnveden (2013) identified the possibility of developing SLCA for a laptop computer and considered new insights that could be obtained, and Martínez-Blanco et al. (2014) explored the chal- lenges of using SLCA to evaluating social performance of fertilizer production. Umair et al. (2015) evaluated recycling of ICT waste, and Dong and Ng (2015) investigated social aspects of constructing a building. These case studies high- light many of the issues that remain to be addressed before SLCA is conducted in the same routine way as practitioners approach ELCA.

The dairy sector plays an important role in agricultural econ- omy of Europe (Kempen et al. 2011) and many ELCA studies have been published detailing the significant environmental impacts of the production chain (Thomassen et al. 2008; O'Brien et al. 2012; Guerci et al. 2013). Much research has focused on evaluating and improving the environmental perfor- mance and economic profitability of dairy farms (Giordano et al. 2011; Yan et al. 2011), but there have been few attempts to investigate the social impacts of dairy farming. There are large differences in dairy farming around the world (Gerber et al. 2013). In Ireland, the dairy production system is a low- cost, grazing-based rotational grazing system, which is different from the confinement systems operating in other European countries. It has been the most profitable farming enterprise in Ireland for the last 40 years (Fitzgerald et al. 2005) and plays a significant role in the agricultural economy. With the abolish- ment of the EU milk quota (Kempen et al. 2011), the output of the Irish dairy industry is expected to increase by 50% by 2020 (DAFM 2010). Irish dairy farming is dominated by family farms, traditionally dependent on family labor (O'Donnell et al. 2008). The Irish dairy sector was motivated by a policy, which aimed to increase productivity by 50% by 2020 (DAFM 2010). This has since been surpassed by a new policy (DAFM 2015), designed to continue productivity increase. While ELCA studies have evaluated the implications of these policies from an environmental perspective (O'Brien et al. 2015) and economic analysis has been undertaken (Shalloo et al. 2014), there has been little social analysis, most of which predates the current policy environment (Miller et al. 2011). In order to evaluate the sustainability of Irish dairy production considering the three pillars of sustainability, the social impacts must be understood. Even with methodological and data shortcomings, a SLCA of current Irish grass-based dairy production is re- quired to establish the baseline against which future SLCA monitoring studies can be compared.
The objective of this work was to evaluate the social im- plication of an average Irish, grass-based dairy farm to provide
a baseline for future work, to identify social hotspots and possible routes to future social sustainability.

In the context of life cycle thinking and the life cycle assessment framework, methods have been developed for the environmental pillar (Environmental Life Cycle Assessment, ELCA) (Finnveden et al. 2009) and the economic pillar (Life Cycle Costing, LCC) (Hoogmartens et al. 2014), but the social pillar is less well developed (Fan et al. 2015).

Social Life Cycle Assessment (SLCA) was derived from social impact assessment (Freudenburg 1986) and followed the structure of ELCA (i) goal and scope definition, (ii) inven- tory analysis, (iii) impact assessment, and (iv) interpretation (ISO 2006a). However, SLCA is quite different in practice. Most of the social impacts in SLCA do not depend on, and are not necessarily proportional to, physical flows but reflect the influence of company behavior with respect to stakeholders (Jorgensen et al. 2008). This leads to the problem of linking impacts to a functional unit, which has been overcome to some extent by combining quantitative and semi-quantitative social indicators for SLCA (Manik et al. 2013; Martínez-
1 UCD School of Biosystems and Food Engineering, Agriculture and Food Science Centre, University College Dublin, Belfield, Dublin, Ireland
Blanco et al. 2014; Dong and Ng 2015) or using the new reference unit "reporting organization" from organization LCA (Martinez-Blanco et al. 2015).

SLCA also demands location-specific data for modeling the system because of the significant cultural and economic differences between countries (Benoît Norris 2013). Suitable high spatial and temporal resolution data are scarce and are not found in many LCA databases (Finnveden et al. 2009), and collecting such data can be quite difficult (Benoît Norris 2013). For example, it is very difficult to collect data like child labor at a company level. So, such data are generally only available at sectoral or national scale (e.g., International Labor Organization (ILO) database) (ILO 2012). In addition, many social impact data are normally expressed in terms of the extent to which best practice is implemented (Dong and Ng 2015). This means building database for SLCA is still at its infancy. The Social Hotspot Database (SHDB) is the most widely used social impact database for SLCA (Ekener- Petersen and Finnveden 2013). However, some indicators in SHDB are based on extrapolation and more sectoral data need to be collected in the future (Martínez-Blanco et al. 2014). In Ireland, the database of the Central Statistics Office is the major source of agricultural data. However, during the aggre- gation of data, some detailed information cannot be derived from this input-output database (Yan et al. 2013a).

In terms of methodology, as the development of SLCA is still at its early stage, there is no standard method for SLCA (Hunkeler 2006; Norris 2006; Weidema 2006; Jorgensen et al. 2008; Dreyer et al. 2010). All of these factors limited SCLA application. To facilitate the SLCA application, recent re- searches focused on solving issues from epistemology to prac- tical application in local level. Instead of discussing methodo- logical issues in diverse SLCA approaches, Iofrida et al. (2016) used the identified research paradigms to justify the diversity of SLCA methodology and suggested an urgent reinforcement of the theoretical bases of SLCA, with focus on epistemological discourse. Zamagni et al. (2015) analyzed different SLCA methodologies and discussed the fundamental differences between ELCA and SLCA. Weidema (2016) proposed a streamlined impacts assessment to reduce data requirement and detailed impact pathway description in practical SLCA and De Luca et al. (2015) adopted a mixed method of qualita- tive technique and multicriteria analysis to identify locally rel- evant social characteristics in agricultural systems.

Currently, more than 70% of SLCA studies (Iofrida et al. 2016) follow the "SLCA Guidelines of the Life Cycle Initiative" of the United Nations Environment Programme and Society of Environmental Toxicology and Chemistry (UNEP-SETAC) (UNEP-SETAC 2009; Benoît-Norris et al. 2011). The "SLCA Guidelines" provide a general framework for conducting a study and classify 31 social issues into five stakeholder categories including "worker," "consumer," "local community," "society," and "value chain factor"

(UNEP-SETAC 2009). Depending on the scope of the study and availability of data, a SLCA should include some or all of the listed social impacts from the "Guidelines" (Benoît et al. 2010). Benoit and her colleagues provide prototypes for conducting SLCA for orange juice and strawberry yogurt (Benoit et al. 2011a, b). Ekener-Petersen and Finnveden (2013) identified the possibility of developing SLCA for a laptop computer and considered new insights that could be obtained, and Martínez-Blanco et al. (2014) explored the chal- lenges of using SLCA to evaluating social performance of fertilizer production. Umair et al. (2015) evaluated recycling of ICT waste, and Dong and Ng (2015) investigated social aspects of constructing a building. These case studies high- light many of the issues that remain to be addressed before SLCA is conducted in the same routine way as practitioners approach ELCA.

The dairy sector plays an important role in agricultural econ- omy of Europe (Kempen et al. 2011) and many ELCA studies have been published detailing the significant environmental impacts of the production chain (Thomassen et al. 2008; O'Brien et al. 2012; Guerci et al. 2013). Much research has focused on evaluating and improving the environmental perfor- mance and economic profitability of dairy farms (Giordano et al. 2011; Yan et al. 2011), but there have been few attempts to investigate the social impacts of dairy farming. There are large differences in dairy farming around the world (Gerber et al. 2013). In Ireland, the dairy production system is a low- cost, grazing-based rotational grazing system, which is different from the confinement systems operating in other European countries. It has been the most profitable farming enterprise in Ireland for the last 40 years (Fitzgerald et al. 2005) and plays a significant role in the agricultural economy. With the abolish- ment of the EU milk quota (Kempen et al. 2011), the output of the Irish dairy industry is expected to increase by 50% by 2020 (DAFM 2010). Irish dairy farming is dominated by family farms, traditionally dependent on family labor (O'Donnell et al. 2008). The Irish dairy sector was motivated by a policy, which aimed to increase productivity by 50% by 2020 (DAFM 2010). This has since been surpassed by a new policy (DAFM 2015), designed to continue productivity increase. While ELCA studies have evaluated the implications of these policies from an environmental perspective (O'Brien et al. 2015) and economic analysis has been undertaken (Shalloo et al. 2014), there has been little social analysis, most of which predates the current policy environment (Miller et al. 2011). In order to evaluate the sustainability of Irish dairy production considering the three pillars of sustainability, the social impacts must be understood. Even with methodological and data shortcomings, a SLCA of current Irish grass-based dairy production is re- quired to establish the baseline against which future SLCA monitoring studies can be compared.
The objective of this work was to evaluate the social im- plication of an average Irish, grass-based dairy farm to provide
a baseline for future work, to identify social hotspots and possible routes to future social sustainability.

12: The SLCA was conducted using the same four stages as ELCA (ISO 2006a) but with definition of human well-being, selection of social indicators, deciding preference for site-specific data or general data, and selection of a method for quantifying the social impacts prior to starting the data collection and analysis (Fan et al. 2015). The definition of human well-being refers not only to human health and safety but also to benefits from hu- man capital, social-economy, social behavior, and culture heri- tage (Weidema 2006; UNEP-SETAC 2009).

Although there is no standard method for SLCA, but the UNEP-SETAC Guideline serves as a suitable framework for SLCA, and has thus far been adopted by a majority of SLCA practioners (Wu et al. 2014). However, there is no consensus in the guideline in terms of impact categories. Two types of impact categories have been proposed: type I aggregates re- sults into groups according to a scoring system and stakehold- er context. Type II impact categories are defined by causal relationships with midpoint and endpoint categories, which are similar to ELCA impact categories. In addition, the guide- line also is constantly evolving, to integrate new thinking such as Global Reporting Initiative's Sustainability Reporting Guidelines, ISO 26000 and Global Social Compliance Programme (Benoît-Norris et al. 2011). In order to ensure

the compatibility of this study with future life cycle sustain- ability assessment (LCSA), the framework of the guideline was followed, with a focus on type II impact categories. Some type I impact categories were also used to present a broad picture of the social impacts of Irish dairy farming.

14: The reason for conducting the study was to evaluate the po- tential social impact caused by the life cycle of Irish milk production up to the farm gate. This was the first attempt to provide an insight into the social impact of Irish dairy farming, and the research findings will offer a context for developing options to improve social performance. The study was aimed at a scientific audience, with policy makers as a secondary interest group. The results were not intended for direct com- parison at this stage, but a standard method was adopted SO the study could serve as a baseline for the future.
15: A typical Irish dairy farm using low-cost, grass-based rota- tional grazing milk production (Fitzgerald et al. 2005) was chosen for modeling over 1 year based on average data from the 2013 National Farm Survey (NFS 2013) (Hanrahan et al. 2014). The system was consistent with that defined for an ELCA of the same average dairy farm (Chen et al. 2016) but modified slightly (Table 1) to reflect the behavior of the orga- nizations involved in the life cycle of the dairy production

19: <header>2.3 Life cycle inventory and activity data </header>

The majority of activity data were obtained from NFS 2013, complemented by other national statistical data from the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine (DAFM), and Central Statistics Office (CSO). The concentrate per cow in 2013 was greater than the previous years due to the weather that year impacting on grass production (Moloney et al. 2014). Compound fertilizer and N fertilizer were calcu- lated based on a 32% increase in P and K use and 21%
Fig. 1 "Cradle to farm gate" system diagram of modeled Irish dairy farm

increase in N use reported for 2013 (Hanrahan et al. 2014; Teagasc 2014). The amount of purchased silage and fertilizer was estimated based on the cost and the average price in 2013 (Central Statistics Office 2013). The characteristics of the av- erage Irish dairy farm in 2013 are summarized in Table 1.

In order to differentiate the dairy from non-dairy activities (e.g., production of heifers for sale), allocation was by the method of (Yan et al. 2013b). All animal numbers were expressed as livestock unit (LU) equivalents based on the ratio of nitrogen excretion compared with a typical dairy cow and the allocation factor as the proportion of all dairy LU to total LU. The allocation between milk and co-product animal live weight was based on the energy and protein requirement of the herd. The proportion of the total energy and protein require- ments of the herd for meat production in an Irish pasture-based system was taken as 12% (Shalloo et al. 2004).

As well as the national average farm level data described, sectoral data were used to derive proxies for sectoral behavior not available at the farm level. These data were sourced from the Healthy and Safety Authority (HSA) (Pat 2013), the Heritage Council (O'Reilly et al. 2005), Bord Bia (Bord 2015), and OECD (OECD 2014) For social information not available at farm or sectoral level, country level data were derived from the Global Competitiveness Report (Schwab and Sala-i-Martín 2013).

These data were sourced from the Healthy and Safety Authority (HSA) (Pat 2013), the Heritage Council (O'Reilly et al. 2005), Bord Bia (Bord 2015), and OECD (OECD 2014) For social information not available at farm or sectoral level, country level data were derived from the Global Competitiveness Report (Schwab and Sala-i-Martín 2013).
21: The "SLCA Guidelines" were followed using the "Methodological Sheets of Subcategories of Impact for a Social LCA" (Benoît-Norris et al. 2011); however, because the system boundary was from cradle to farm gate, the con- sumer stakeholder category was not included. The impacted stakeholders were limited to worker, local community, society, and value chain actors (UNEP-SETAC 2009), with 19 subcat- egories selected (Table 2) based on data availability. As farm owners and family labor are the main labor source, their over- lapping roles can lead to some bias (Manik et al. 2013). The impacts "health and safety" and "working hours" were direct- ly related to the employer SO to avoid risk of bias (Benoît Norris 2013); owners and family members were defined in the "worker" category but excluded from the "fair salary" and "local employment" subcategories. To avoid double counting in the "health and safety" subcategory, lost working days due to ill health caused by environmental impacts were excluded.

As it is not possible to link all social indicators to the prod- uct in a quantitative way, the social impact categories were divided into three groups: group 1, quantitative and relatable to the functional unit; group 2, quantitative categories but not relatable to the functional unit; and group 3, semi-quantitative. Subcategories in group 1 were type II impact categories, while the ones in groups 2 and 3 were type II impact categories.

For group 1, the Life Cycle Working Environment (LCWE) database supplied by Gabi 6 (Thinkstep 2014) was used to calculate health and safety and working hours. LCWE is mainly derived from US statistics with country-specific correction for each process using a top- down approach (Beck et al. 2009, PE-International 2010). It is valid only for countries holding similar eco- nomic and socioeconomic conditions than the USA (Martínez-Blanco et al. 2014). It contains sectoral back- ground information about working time (seconds/per product) and lethal and non-lethal occupational acci- dents. The foreground information about accidents and working time on dairy farms was derived from HSA and NFS, respectively. Stepwise 2006 (Weidema 2009) was used to aggregate all environmental impacts to hu- man health into a single unit by monetarization. For this study, only the cost value of environmental impacts on human well-being was used to assess the social indica- tor "safe and healthy working conditions." For group 1 indicators, the normalization was calculated based on the contribution of impacts from on-farm activities (Table 2).

For group 2, two indicators "hiring and firing practices" and "freedom of association and collective bargaining" were chosen to evaluate working benefit. They were derived from the competitiveness index of the World Economic Forum (WEF) at country level. All performances were scored from 1 (worst) to 7 (best). The value range of 1 to 7 from WEF was normalized into range of value from (worst) to 1 (best). Old age labor and young labor were used for assessing age struc- ture. The percentage of old labor (65+) and young labor (<35) over all types of Irish farms is 25 and 7%, respectively. These values were used for indicating an "average performance" (normalized value = 0) in age structure. More than 50% of old labor and 14% of young labor were considered as the worst performance in old labor and best performance in young labor indicators, respectively. The share of female workers in agriculture was used to assess equal opportunity. According to experience, the share of female worker in agricultural sector is usually <50%, SO the best scenario was equal share (50%) of job by women with a normalized value of "1" and the worst scenario was zero share of jobs by women with a normalized value of "-1." The percentage between 0 and 50% were nor- malized into a range of values from -1 to 1. The difference between real national minimum hourly wage and statutory minimum hourly wage (€ 1.65) was used for evaluating fair wage. If the difference between average hourly salary for dairy workers and the real national minimum hourly wage was greater than € 1.65, the value for fair salary was normalized to -1 (negative scenario) or 1 (positive scenario). If average hourly salary for dairy workers matched the real national min- imum hourly wage, the normalized value for fair wage was "0." The percentage of the farms using manure spreading and

No. 164 (Statutory Instruments 2010); LCWE Life Cycle Working Environment database (Beck et al. 2009; PE-International 2010), NFS National Farm Survey (Hanrahan et al. 2014), CSO Central Statistics Office (CSO. 2013), HAS Healthy and Safety Authority (Pat 2013), WEF World Economic Forum (Schwab and Sala-i-Martín 2013), Heritage council (O'Reilly et al. 2005), Stepwise 2006 (Weidema 2009), FBD survey (Amy 2016), ICBF (ICBF 2012), Bord Bia (Bord 2015), IFA (IFA 2013) a Semi-quantitative b Quantitative-functional c Quantitative-non-functional

storage technique was used to assess "respect indigenous rights" as manure and related odor are the most "disturbing elements" for the local community. Indicators in groups 2 and 3 were normalized in a continuous value range (-1 to 1), except for local employment (0 to 1). Six semi-quantitative indicators were included in group 3. The social performance was assessed according to "whether the social scheme is reached or exists." Only two values were available after normalization: 1 (positive), or -1 (negative). Detail of the normalization is provided in Table 2. For "technology development," the evaluation reference was Economic Breeding Index (EBI), which is a profit index for helping farmers identify the most profitable bulls and cows for breeding dairy herd replacements. EBI can be increased by improving management or genetic technology, SO production efficiency can be achieved by increasing EBI (Berry et al. 2007). For "supplier relationship," a stable supply relationship was considered positive, SO extent of milk supply agreement (MSA) adoption was the assessment criterion, derived from Irish Farmers' Association 2013). "Accessing to written contracts" and "statutory requirement for protection and maintenance of heritage" were used for evaluating the "working condition transparency" and "natural and cultural heritage" performance. The material for assessment was from Irish government files (statutory instruments and heritage council). Training opportunities and a "green scheme" for dairy products were used for assessing "professional development" and "promoting social responsibility" indica- tors. The supporting information was from the Irish Agricultural and Food Authority (Teagasc) and the Irish Food Board (Bord Bia).

No. 164 (Statutory Instruments 2010); LCWE Life Cycle Working Environment database (Beck et al. 2009; PE-International 2010), NFS National Farm Survey (Hanrahan et al. 2014), CSO Central Statistics Office (CSO. 2013), HAS Healthy and Safety Authority (Pat 2013), WEF World Economic Forum (Schwab and Sala-i-Martín 2013), Heritage council (O'Reilly et al. 2005), Stepwise 2006 (Weidema 2009), FBD survey (Amy 2016), ICBF (ICBF 2012), Bord Bia (Bord 2015), IFA (IFA 2013) a Semi-quantitative b Quantitative-functional c Quantitative-non-functional

storage technique was used to assess "respect indigenous rights" as manure and related odor are the most "disturbing elements" for the local community. Indicators in groups 2 and 3 were normalized in a continuous value range (-1 to 1), except for local employment (0 to 1). Six semi-quantitative indicators were included in group 3. The social performance was assessed according to "whether the social scheme is reached or exists." Only two values were available after normalization: 1 (positive), or -1 (negative). Detail of the normalization is provided in Table 2. For "technology development," the evaluation reference was Economic Breeding Index (EBI), which is a profit index for helping farmers identify the most profitable bulls and cows for breeding dairy herd replacements. EBI can be increased by improving management or genetic technology, SO production efficiency can be achieved by increasing EBI (Berry et al. 2007). For "supplier relationship," a stable supply relationship was considered positive, SO extent of milk supply agreement (MSA) adoption was the assessment criterion, derived from Irish Farmers' Association 2013). "Accessing to written contracts" and "statutory requirement for protection and maintenance of heritage" were used for evaluating the "working condition transparency" and "natural and cultural heritage" performance. The material for assessment was from Irish government files (statutory instruments and heritage council). Training opportunities and a "green scheme" for dairy products were used for assessing "professional development" and "promoting social responsibility" indica- tors. The supporting information was from the Irish Agricultural and Food Authority (Teagasc) and the Irish Food Board (Bord Bia).
23: The "SLCA Guidelines" were followed using the "Methodological Sheets of Subcategories of Impact for a Social LCA" (Benoît-Norris et al. 2011); however, because the system boundary was from cradle to farm gate, the con- sumer stakeholder category was not included. The impacted stakeholders were limited to worker, local community, society, and value chain actors (UNEP-SETAC 2009), with 19 subcat- egories selected (Table 2) based on data availability. As farm owners and family labor are the main labor source, their over- lapping roles can lead to some bias (Manik et al. 2013). The impacts "health and safety" and "working hours" were direct- ly related to the employer SO to avoid risk of bias (Benoît Norris 2013); owners and family members were defined in the "worker" category but excluded from the "fair salary" and "local employment" subcategories. To avoid double counting in the "health and safety" subcategory, lost working days due to ill health caused by environmental impacts were excluded.

As it is not possible to link all social indicators to the prod- uct in a quantitative way, the social impact categories were divided into three groups: group 1, quantitative and relatable to the functional unit; group 2, quantitative categories but not relatable to the functional unit; and group 3, semi-quantitative. Subcategories in group 1 were type II impact categories, while the ones in groups 2 and 3 were type II impact categories.

For group 1, the Life Cycle Working Environment (LCWE) database supplied by Gabi 6 (Thinkstep 2014) was used to calculate health and safety and working hours. LCWE is mainly derived from US statistics with country-specific correction for each process using a top- down approach (Beck et al. 2009, PE-International 2010). It is valid only for countries holding similar eco- nomic and socioeconomic conditions than the USA (Martínez-Blanco et al. 2014). It contains sectoral back- ground information about working time (seconds/per product) and lethal and non-lethal occupational acci- dents. The foreground information about accidents and working time on dairy farms was derived from HSA and NFS, respectively. Stepwise 2006 (Weidema 2009) was used to aggregate all environmental impacts to hu- man health into a single unit by monetarization. For this study, only the cost value of environmental impacts on human well-being was used to assess the social indica- tor "safe and healthy working conditions." For group 1 indicators, the normalization was calculated based on the contribution of impacts from on-farm activities (Table 2).

For group 2, two indicators "hiring and firing practices" and "freedom of association and collective bargaining" were chosen to evaluate working benefit. They were derived from the competitiveness index of the World Economic Forum (WEF) at country level. All performances were scored from 1 (worst) to 7 (best). The value range of 1 to 7 from WEF was normalized into range of value from (worst) to 1 (best). Old age labor and young labor were used for assessing age struc- ture. The percentage of old labor (65+) and young labor (<35) over all types of Irish farms is 25 and 7%, respectively. These values were used for indicating an "average performance" (normalized value = 0) in age structure. More than 50% of old labor and 14% of young labor were considered as the worst performance in old labor and best performance in young labor indicators, respectively. The share of female workers in agriculture was used to assess equal opportunity. According to experience, the share of female worker in agricultural sector is usually <50%, SO the best scenario was equal share (50%) of job by women with a normalized value of "1" and the worst scenario was zero share of jobs by women with a normalized value of "-1." The percentage between 0 and 50% were nor- malized into a range of values from -1 to 1. The difference between real national minimum hourly wage and statutory minimum hourly wage (€ 1.65) was used for evaluating fair wage. If the difference between average hourly salary for dairy workers and the real national minimum hourly wage was greater than € 1.65, the value for fair salary was normalized to -1 (negative scenario) or 1 (positive scenario). If average hourly salary for dairy workers matched the real national min- imum hourly wage, the normalized value for fair wage was "0." The percentage of the farms using manure spreading and

No. 164 (Statutory Instruments 2010); LCWE Life Cycle Working Environment database (Beck et al. 2009; PE-International 2010), NFS National Farm Survey (Hanrahan et al. 2014), CSO Central Statistics Office (CSO. 2013), HAS Healthy and Safety Authority (Pat 2013), WEF World Economic Forum (Schwab and Sala-i-Martín 2013), Heritage council (O'Reilly et al. 2005), Stepwise 2006 (Weidema 2009), FBD survey (Amy 2016), ICBF (ICBF 2012), Bord Bia (Bord 2015), IFA (IFA 2013) a Semi-quantitative b Quantitative-functional c Quantitative-non-functional

storage technique was used to assess "respect indigenous rights" as manure and related odor are the most "disturbing elements" for the local community. Indicators in groups 2 and 3 were normalized in a continuous value range (-1 to 1), except for local employment (0 to 1). Six semi-quantitative indicators were included in group 3. The social performance was assessed according to "whether the social scheme is reached or exists." Only two values were available after normalization: 1 (positive), or -1 (negative). Detail of the normalization is provided in Table 2. For "technology development," the evaluation reference was Economic Breeding Index (EBI), which is a profit index for helping farmers identify the most profitable bulls and cows for breeding dairy herd replacements. EBI can be increased by improving management or genetic technology, SO production efficiency can be achieved by increasing EBI (Berry et al. 2007). For "supplier relationship," a stable supply relationship was considered positive, SO extent of milk supply agreement (MSA) adoption was the assessment criterion, derived from Irish Farmers' Association 2013). "Accessing to written contracts" and "statutory requirement for protection and maintenance of heritage" were used for evaluating the "working condition transparency" and "natural and cultural heritage" performance. The material for assessment was from Irish government files (statutory instruments and heritage council). Training opportunities and a "green scheme" for dairy products were used for assessing "professional development" and "promoting social responsibility" indica- tors. The supporting information was from the Irish Agricultural and Food Authority (Teagasc) and the Irish Food Board (Bord Bia).
26: The "SLCA Guidelines" were followed using the "Methodological Sheets of Subcategories of Impact for a Social LCA" (Benoît-Norris et al. 2011); however, because the system boundary was from cradle to farm gate, the con- sumer stakeholder category was not included. The impacted stakeholders were limited to worker, local community, society, and value chain actors (UNEP-SETAC 2009), with 19 subcat- egories selected (Table 2) based on data availability. As farm owners and family labor are the main labor source, their over- lapping roles can lead to some bias (Manik et al. 2013). The impacts "health and safety" and "working hours" were direct- ly related to the employer SO to avoid risk of bias (Benoît Norris 2013); owners and family members were defined in the "worker" category but excluded from the "fair salary" and "local employment" subcategories. To avoid double counting in the "health and safety" subcategory, lost working days due to ill health caused by environmental impacts were excluded.

As it is not possible to link all social indicators to the prod- uct in a quantitative way, the social impact categories were divided into three groups: group 1, quantitative and relatable to the functional unit; group 2, quantitative categories but not relatable to the functional unit; and group 3, semi-quantitative. Subcategories in group 1 were type II impact categories, while the ones in groups 2 and 3 were type II impact categories.

For group 1, the Life Cycle Working Environment (LCWE) database supplied by Gabi 6 (Thinkstep 2014) was used to calculate health and safety and working hours. LCWE is mainly derived from US statistics with country-specific correction for each process using a top- down approach (Beck et al. 2009, PE-International 2010). It is valid only for countries holding similar eco- nomic and socioeconomic conditions than the USA (Martínez-Blanco et al. 2014). It contains sectoral back- ground information about working time (seconds/per product) and lethal and non-lethal occupational acci- dents. The foreground information about accidents and working time on dairy farms was derived from HSA and NFS, respectively. Stepwise 2006 (Weidema 2009) was used to aggregate all environmental impacts to hu- man health into a single unit by monetarization. For this study, only the cost value of environmental impacts on human well-being was used to assess the social indica- tor "safe and healthy working conditions." For group 1 indicators, the normalization was calculated based on the contribution of impacts from on-farm activities (Table 2).

For group 2, two indicators "hiring and firing practices" and "freedom of association and collective bargaining" were chosen to evaluate working benefit. They were derived from the competitiveness index of the World Economic Forum (WEF) at country level. All performances were scored from 1 (worst) to 7 (best). The value range of 1 to 7 from WEF was normalized into range of value from (worst) to 1 (best). Old age labor and young labor were used for assessing age struc- ture. The percentage of old labor (65+) and young labor (<35) over all types of Irish farms is 25 and 7%, respectively. These values were used for indicating an "average performance" (normalized value = 0) in age structure. More than 50% of old labor and 14% of young labor were considered as the worst performance in old labor and best performance in young labor indicators, respectively. The share of female workers in agriculture was used to assess equal opportunity. According to experience, the share of female worker in agricultural sector is usually <50%, SO the best scenario was equal share (50%) of job by women with a normalized value of "1" and the worst scenario was zero share of jobs by women with a normalized value of "-1." The percentage between 0 and 50% were nor- malized into a range of values from -1 to 1. The difference between real national minimum hourly wage and statutory minimum hourly wage (€ 1.65) was used for evaluating fair wage. If the difference between average hourly salary for dairy workers and the real national minimum hourly wage was greater than € 1.65, the value for fair salary was normalized to -1 (negative scenario) or 1 (positive scenario). If average hourly salary for dairy workers matched the real national min- imum hourly wage, the normalized value for fair wage was "0." The percentage of the farms using manure spreading and

No. 164 (Statutory Instruments 2010); LCWE Life Cycle Working Environment database (Beck et al. 2009; PE-International 2010), NFS National Farm Survey (Hanrahan et al. 2014), CSO Central Statistics Office (CSO. 2013), HAS Healthy and Safety Authority (Pat 2013), WEF World Economic Forum (Schwab and Sala-i-Martín 2013), Heritage council (O'Reilly et al. 2005), Stepwise 2006 (Weidema 2009), FBD survey (Amy 2016), ICBF (ICBF 2012), Bord Bia (Bord 2015), IFA (IFA 2013) a Semi-quantitative b Quantitative-functional c Quantitative-non-functional

storage technique was used to assess "respect indigenous rights" as manure and related odor are the most "disturbing elements" for the local community. Indicators in groups 2 and 3 were normalized in a continuous value range (-1 to 1), except for local employment (0 to 1). Six semi-quantitative indicators were included in group 3. The social performance was assessed according to "whether the social scheme is reached or exists." Only two values were available after normalization: 1 (positive), or -1 (negative). Detail of the normalization is provided in Table 2. For "technology development," the evaluation reference was Economic Breeding Index (EBI), which is a profit index for helping farmers identify the most profitable bulls and cows for breeding dairy herd replacements. EBI can be increased by improving management or genetic technology, SO production efficiency can be achieved by increasing EBI (Berry et al. 2007). For "supplier relationship," a stable supply relationship was considered positive, SO extent of milk supply agreement (MSA) adoption was the assessment criterion, derived from Irish Farmers' Association 2013). "Accessing to written contracts" and "statutory requirement for protection and maintenance of heritage" were used for evaluating the "working condition transparency" and "natural and cultural heritage" performance. The material for assessment was from Irish government files (statutory instruments and heritage council). Training opportunities and a "green scheme" for dairy products were used for assessing "professional development" and "promoting social responsibility" indica- tors. The supporting information was from the Irish Agricultural and Food Authority (Teagasc) and the Irish Food Board (Bord Bia).
29: The "SLCA Guidelines" were followed using the "Methodological Sheets of Subcategories of Impact for a Social LCA" (Benoît-Norris et al. 2011); however, because the system boundary was from cradle to farm gate, the con- sumer stakeholder category was not included. The impacted stakeholders were limited to worker, local community, society, and value chain actors (UNEP-SETAC 2009), with 19 subcat- egories selected (Table 2) based on data availability. As farm owners and family labor are the main labor source, their over- lapping roles can lead to some bias (Manik et al. 2013). The impacts "health and safety" and "working hours" were direct- ly related to the employer SO to avoid risk of bias (Benoît Norris 2013); owners and family members were defined in the "worker" category but excluded from the "fair salary" and "local employment" subcategories. To avoid double counting in the "health and safety" subcategory, lost working days due to ill health caused by environmental impacts were excluded.

As it is not possible to link all social indicators to the prod- uct in a quantitative way, the social impact categories were divided into three groups: group 1, quantitative and relatable to the functional unit; group 2, quantitative categories but not relatable to the functional unit; and group 3, semi-quantitative. Subcategories in group 1 were type II impact categories, while the ones in groups 2 and 3 were type II impact categories.

For group 1, the Life Cycle Working Environment (LCWE) database supplied by Gabi 6 (Thinkstep 2014) was used to calculate health and safety and working hours. LCWE is mainly derived from US statistics with country-specific correction for each process using a top- down approach (Beck et al. 2009, PE-International 2010). It is valid only for countries holding similar eco- nomic and socioeconomic conditions than the USA (Martínez-Blanco et al. 2014). It contains sectoral back- ground information about working time (seconds/per product) and lethal and non-lethal occupational acci- dents. The foreground information about accidents and working time on dairy farms was derived from HSA and NFS, respectively. Stepwise 2006 (Weidema 2009) was used to aggregate all environmental impacts to hu- man health into a single unit by monetarization. For this study, only the cost value of environmental impacts on human well-being was used to assess the social indica- tor "safe and healthy working conditions." For group 1 indicators, the normalization was calculated based on the contribution of impacts from on-farm activities (Table 2).

For group 2, two indicators "hiring and firing practices" and "freedom of association and collective bargaining" were chosen to evaluate working benefit. They were derived from the competitiveness index of the World Economic Forum (WEF) at country level. All performances were scored from 1 (worst) to 7 (best). The value range of 1 to 7 from WEF was normalized into range of value from (worst) to 1 (best). Old age labor and young labor were used for assessing age struc- ture. The percentage of old labor (65+) and young labor (<35) over all types of Irish farms is 25 and 7%, respectively. These values were used for indicating an "average performance" (normalized value = 0) in age structure. More than 50% of old labor and 14% of young labor were considered as the worst performance in old labor and best performance in young labor indicators, respectively. The share of female workers in agriculture was used to assess equal opportunity. According to experience, the share of female worker in agricultural sector is usually <50%, SO the best scenario was equal share (50%) of job by women with a normalized value of "1" and the worst scenario was zero share of jobs by women with a normalized value of "-1." The percentage between 0 and 50% were nor- malized into a range of values from -1 to 1. The difference between real national minimum hourly wage and statutory minimum hourly wage (€ 1.65) was used for evaluating fair wage. If the difference between average hourly salary for dairy workers and the real national minimum hourly wage was greater than € 1.65, the value for fair salary was normalized to -1 (negative scenario) or 1 (positive scenario). If average hourly salary for dairy workers matched the real national min- imum hourly wage, the normalized value for fair wage was "0." The percentage of the farms using manure spreading and

31: The normalized value of each social impact indicator (Fig. showed that 14 of the 19 indicators were >0 for the average Irish dairy farm, SO overall, it can be concluded that the farms in this sector make a positive social contribution. There were a number of indicators that were only just positive. In order to maintain its positive social influence, these social issues will need careful management with dairy expansion targets (DAFM 2015) over the coming decade. The most negative social contribution was found in health and safety indicators, followed by public health and safe living conditions, equal opportunity, and working hours.

The impact on health and safe living conditions (from a society point of view, not the worker) showed that the envi- ronmental impacts generated up to the farm gate to produce 1 kg ECM cost €0.101 in human health. The contribution analysis (Table 3) indicated that the main causes of impact on health and living conditions came from inorganic respira- tory effects (51.9%), human toxicity carcinogens (44.5%), and global warming (2.3%). Along the supply chain to the farm gate, 34.3% of total impact on health and safe living condi- tions was from on-farm activities, including milk production, manure storage and application, animal excretion, grass man- agement, and fertilizer spreading, followed by fertilizer pro- duction (38.4%) and off-farm feed production (26.2%). Disaggregated impacts on working time and health and safety (Table 4) showed that farm activity also accounted for a large part of health and safety impact. Per 1 kg ECM, the fatality accident rate was estimated to be 2.5E -09 with almost 95% of lethal accidents happening on-farm. The average total

33: The normalized value of each social impact indicator (Fig. showed that 14 of the 19 indicators were >0 for the average Irish dairy farm, SO overall, it can be concluded that the farms in this sector make a positive social contribution. There were a number of indicators that were only just positive. In order to maintain its positive social influence, these social issues will need careful management with dairy expansion targets (DAFM 2015) over the coming decade. The most negative social contribution was found in health and safety indicators, followed by public health and safe living conditions, equal opportunity, and working hours.

The impact on health and safe living conditions (from a society point of view, not the worker) showed that the envi- ronmental impacts generated up to the farm gate to produce 1 kg ECM cost €0.101 in human health. The contribution analysis (Table 3) indicated that the main causes of impact on health and living conditions came from inorganic respira- tory effects (51.9%), human toxicity carcinogens (44.5%), and global warming (2.3%). Along the supply chain to the farm gate, 34.3% of total impact on health and safe living condi- tions was from on-farm activities, including milk production, manure storage and application, animal excretion, grass man- agement, and fertilizer spreading, followed by fertilizer pro- duction (38.4%) and off-farm feed production (26.2%). Disaggregated impacts on working time and health and safety (Table 4) showed that farm activity also accounted for a large part of health and safety impact. Per 1 kg ECM, the fatality accident rate was estimated to be 2.5E -09 with almost 95% of lethal accidents happening on-farm. The average total

36: The majority of social impact indicators for worker stake- holders were positive. A fair wage, social benefits, and secu- rity can protect the rights of dairy farm workers (Schwab and Sala-i-Martín 2013) and reduce the number of dairy workers

leaving the sector (DAFM 2009). Transparent working condi- tions (Statutory Instruments 2010), freedom of association, and collective bargaining (Schwab and Sala-i-Martín 2013) secure an appropriate labor-employer relationship on the dairy farm. However, it is noteworthy that the data from the World Economic Forum dataset was at country level. A sectoral level survey would be needed to improve the accuracy for future social performance studies. A well-established professional development system at university, institution, and company level enables dairy workers to improve their professional skills and keeps them in touch with the latest farming tech- niques (Dillon et al. 2016). Compared to other agricultural sectors, the low percentage of old age labor and large amount
Table 4 Contribution analysis of impact on "working time" and "health and safety"

Item|Working time/ECM (second)|Working time/ECM (second)|Fatality rate/ECM|Fatality rate/ECM Item|Value|%|Value|% On farm activity|26.12|58.17%|2.37E-09|94.80% P/K fertilizer production|0.86|1.92%|3.22E-12|0.13% CAN production|3.69|8.22%|2.82E-11|1.13% Diesel production|0.32|0.72%|2.48E-11|0.99% Water supply|9.92|22.09%|6.62E-11|2.65% Electricity generation|1.36|3.03%|1.16E-11|0.46% Transportation|0.06|0.14%|4.69E-14|0.00% Purchased silage production|0.13|0.29%|N/A|N/A Concentrate production|1.78|3.96%|1.32E-11|0.53% Manure storage facility|0.60|1.33%|4.01E-12|0.16% Total|44.90||2.5E-09|

of young labor indicated that the age structure in dairy farming will favor its ongoing expansion and development. New en- trant dairy farmers can more easily adopt improving technol- ogy and farm financial management (McDonald et al. 2016). This labor structure will maximize the possibility of new tech- nologies being adopted on dairy farms. The percentage of young dairy farmers has declined since the 2003 Farm Structures Survey, when there were 13% of dairy farmers <35 years of age in 2003 (DAFM 2009), SO more attention and effort will be needed to maintain or increase the number of new entrant dairy farmers. Unsurprisingly, the negative per- formance of equal opportunity in dairy farming suggested that this sector is still dominated by male workers, like other agri- cultural sectors. It has been suggested that the reason is female workers have a higher risk of injury in doing livestock-related work (Kallioniemi et al. 2011), but a recent study found the percentage of female workers in the dairy sector is increasing (Brandth 2002), SO the result may be related to family struc- tures and ownership. In order to improve the safety of the working environment and increase the number of female dairy workers, new production technologies (e.g., automatic milking systems) can be introduced to reduce direct contact with animals.

The health and safety impacts confirmed previous findings that dairy farming is among the most hazardous for workers (Kallioniemi et al. 2011). The major causes of fatality on dairy farms are associated with heavy equipment, manure-handling, and livestock handling (Douphrate et al. 2013). In addition, Irish dairy farmers also have a greater risk of injury or disabil- ity, e.g., work-related musculoskeletal disorders, due to the nature of the work, and the land and long working hours (Osborne et al. 2013). These impacts could cause a sequence of negative effects like reduction of family farm income, ces- sation of off-farm employment, and a decline of product qual- ity due to a lack of support (Whelan et al. 2009). The expan- sion of Irish dairy farming could aggravate the health and safety performance, especially for large dairy farms unless appropriate labor management is put in place. Considering the high on-farm accident rate, simply being more careful and adding simple safety measures (HSA 2011) may be not enough to eradicate the problem. A less labor-intensive pro- duction system like robotic milking (Hansen 2015) might im- prove health and safety performance by reducing on-farm workload and contact with animals.

From a worker perspective, dairy farming is a time- consuming sector with the activities on the farm accounting for more than half of working time (Table 4). The annual labor input per cow can be 99 h for a small Irish dairy farm and 41 h for larger farm 'Shea et al. 1988). Although labor efficiency has increased at a rate of approximately 1 h per cow per year, this is still regarded as an insufficient improvement given the rapid growth of sector to meet market demand (O'Donovan et al. 2008). The efficiency of some labor-consuming

processes like milking and calf management needs to improve in order to increase productivity. Farmers could adopt less labor-intensive management by using ICT supported manage- ment (Jago et al. 2013; Rutten et al. 2013) or more efficient machines and systems, e.g., automatic milk systems (Davis et al. 2008). These systems could potentially change the labor type, as farmers will spend more time on managing and over- seeing the system than doing the manual work (Shortall et al. 2015).

In order to improve the safety of the working environment and increase the number of female dairy workers, new production technologies (e.g., automatic milking systems) can be introduced to reduce direct contact with animals.

The health and safety impacts confirmed previous findings that dairy farming is among the most hazardous for workers (Kallioniemi et al. 2011). The major causes of fatality on dairy farms are associated with heavy equipment, manure-handling, and livestock handling (Douphrate et al. 2013). In addition, Irish dairy farmers also have a greater risk of injury or disabil- ity, e.g., work-related musculoskeletal disorders, due to the nature of the work, and the land and long working hours (Osborne et al. 2013). These impacts could cause a sequence of negative effects like reduction of family farm income, ces- sation of off-farm employment, and a decline of product qual- ity due to a lack of support (Whelan et al. 2009). The expan- sion of Irish dairy farming could aggravate the health and safety performance, especially for large dairy farms unless appropriate labor management is put in place. Considering the high on-farm accident rate, simply being more careful and adding simple safety measures (HSA 2011) may be not enough to eradicate the problem. A less labor-intensive pro- duction system like robotic milking (Hansen 2015) might im- prove health and safety performance by reducing on-farm workload and contact with animals.

From a worker perspective, dairy farming is a time- consuming sector with the activities on the farm accounting for more than half of working time (Table 4). The annual labor input per cow can be 99 h for a small Irish dairy farm and 41 h for larger farm 'Shea et al. 1988). Although labor efficiency has increased at a rate of approximately 1 h per cow per year, this is still regarded as an insufficient improvement given the rapid growth of sector to meet market demand (O'Donovan et al. 2008). The efficiency of some labor-consuming

processes like milking and calf management needs to improve in order to increase productivity. Farmers could adopt less labor-intensive management by using ICT supported manage- ment (Jago et al. 2013; Rutten et al. 2013) or more efficient machines and systems, e.g., automatic milk systems (Davis et al. 2008). These systems could potentially change the labor type, as farmers will spend more time on managing and over- seeing the system than doing the manual work (Shortall et al. 2015).
40: The majority of social impact indicators for worker stake- holders were positive. A fair wage, social benefits, and secu- rity can protect the rights of dairy farm workers (Schwab and Sala-i-Martín 2013) and reduce the number of dairy workers

leaving the sector (DAFM 2009). Transparent working condi- tions (Statutory Instruments 2010), freedom of association, and collective bargaining (Schwab and Sala-i-Martín 2013) secure an appropriate labor-employer relationship on the dairy farm. However, it is noteworthy that the data from the World Economic Forum dataset was at country level. A sectoral level survey would be needed to improve the accuracy for future social performance studies. A well-established professional development system at university, institution, and company level enables dairy workers to improve their professional skills and keeps them in touch with the latest farming tech- niques (Dillon et al. 2016). Compared to other agricultural sectors, the low percentage of old age labor and large amount

41: Local community stakeholders appear to attain a positive so- cial impact from dairy farming, except for "health and safe living conditions." Due to the well-established guideline for heritage protection (O'Reilly et al. 2005), high rate of manure storage and process facility on Irish dairy farms (96%) and the high sense of community connection (88%) among Irish dairy farmers (Amy 2016), indicators of natural and culture heri- tage, community engagement, and respect for indigenous rights were strongly positive. This social performance can facilitate expansion of the Irish dairy sector without jeopardiz- ing the interests of the local community. In terms of local employment, Irish dairy farms only contributed 2% of work- ing opportunities in the agriculture labor market. Consider the large share of milk sales (28%) in the Irish agriculture econo- my (Central Statistics Office 2014), dairy farms do not offer many working opportunities. One of the main reasons is many Irish dairy farms are family owned (O'Donnell et al. 2008) and family labor does not appear in labor statistics.

The results for health and safe living conditions implied that the majority of environmental impacts occurred on-farm, which is supported by the results of (O'Brien et al. 2012; Guerci et al. 2013). These environmental burdens can lead to impacts on human health. Weidema and his colleagues (Weidema and Eder 2008) calculated that the total environ- mental impact of 1 kg raw milk produced by the EU dairy sector could cause €0.148 worth of damage to human health. Although the environmental impacts from Irish dairy farms cause less damage to public health than the EU average, on- farm activities are one of the major environmental hotspot in the life cycle of Irish milk production. In terms of impact pathway, ammonia and nitrogen oxide of on-farm (31.7%) activities and fertilizer and feed productions of off-farm (19.4%) are the main drivers of the modeled inorganic respi- ratory effects. Hydrocarbons from fertilizer production and associated fertilizer for feed production are the main pollutants for carcinogenic human toxicity, which was dominated by nitrogen fertilizer production (26.6%) for dairy farming. The majority of on-farm-related health costs were associated with respiratory impacts from manure management. As a local ef- fect, ammonia and nitrogen oxide emissions from manure on dairy farms can cause damage to the local community,

especially to people living around a dairy farm. Based on annual milk production of 5.4 billion liters in 013(CSO; 2013), on-farm related health impacts (34% of total health impact) could be estimated to cause an approximate public health cost of €187 million, which was around 1.4% of the health budget for 2013 (Department of Finance 2013). This impact could increase with the expansion of dairy sector, SO in order to improve health and safe living condition, Irish dairy farmers should minimize on-farm-related health costs by re- ducing ammonia and nitrogen emissions from manure. This could be achieved by improving manure storage or adopting advanced manure process facilities like aerobic storage system (O'Brien e et al. 2012).

44: The indicators "technology development" and "economic contribution" suggested that Irish dairy farms make signifi- cant contributions to society. The economic contribution of the dairy sector to the agricultural economy was 28% in 2013 (CSO 2013) After the abolishment of EU milk quota, the primary output value of milk was expected to increase by €700 M (DAFM 2010). The result in Fig. 2 shows that raw milk production in 2013 had achieved >86% of this goal. Dairy farming also brings benefits to other stakeholders in the supply chain, due to the greater increase in value-added dairy food products (DAFM 2010). In terms of technology development, the EBI has helped to maintain a 7% annual increase in recent years. This technology achievement has improved the production efficiency of Irish dairy farms and reduced production costs (Berry et al. 2005). However, some technology developments can reduce labor intensive work, which might come at a social cost, as the working opportuni- ties are reduced (Alvarez-Cuadrado and Poschke 2011).

The two semi-quantitative indicators for the value chain stakeholders also indicated positive social impacts. The social responsibility of stakeholders in the Irish milk production chain is improving through adoption of eco-labels (e.g., Carbon Trust certification and the national scheme, "Origin Green," initiated by Bord Bia. These schemes certify the environmental impacts of stakeholders involved in Irish milk production chain and help them to improve the environmental perfor- mance of their products (Brécard et al. 2009). For sup- plier relationships, according to IFA, most of dairy farmers sign a milk supply agreement (MSA) with a dairy processor. A MSA can help to stabilize the supply of milk and reduce price fluctuation between dairy farmers and processors. This trading mechanism can lower the risk of failure of the Irish milk market by increasing farmers' participation in the supply chain and reducing the dominance of processors in the market (Gorton et al. 2006).

46: There are few SLCA of milk production, and most previous research has used type I impact categories for the valuation method (Revéret et al. 2015). The indicators cannot be readily related to the functional unit in a quantitative manner, which can lead to problems when integrating the results into LCSA with the same functional unit as the environmental and eco- nomic components. This study used mixed methodology to evaluate the social performance of the average Irish dairy farm, with a focus on type II impact indicators (group 1). These indicators have a quantitative causal relationship with the functional unit and can be easily integrated into LCSA (Neugebauer et al. 2015). In addition, previous SLCA studies of milk production have focused on confinement dairy sys- tems (Revéret et al. 2015), but this research has provided important new insight into the social performance of grass- based, rotational grazing dairy systems. The results of this study include several new indicators for evaluating social per- formance of dairy systems and has also confirmed some of the qualitative findings from previous research (Revéret et al. 2015) that dairy farms could face social problems in relation to working hours and health and safety.

However, due to the limits of current SLCA methodology and available databases, it is difficult to link all social indica- tors to the functional unit in a quantitative way. The majority of indicators in this study were in group 2 and group 3, and for semi-quantitative indicators, the valuation process is similar to life cycle attribute assessment (Wu et al. 2014), when only two values (-1 or 1) are assigned to the indicators. This could mask any improvement in social behavior. The sectoral level semi-quantitative indicators could be replaced by more specif- ic indicators in a SLCA study of individual farms for future work. A future survey of dairy stakeholders could be used to define weighting factors for all social indicators (Manik et al. 2013; Dong and Ng 2015) to calculate an aggregated endpoint indicator.

48: It can be concluded that the average Irish dairy farming was having a positive social impact for most stakeholders in 2013. Strong positive social impacts were found for value chain actors and society, predominantly positive impacts for local community and generally positive values for the worker. There were three very negative impacts: health and safety and equal opportunity/discrimination for workers and safe and healthy living conditions for the local community. It can be expected that these negative social hotspots might be ex- acerbated with expansion of the Irish dairy sector unless targeted policy is put in place to reduce their impact.

49: It can be concluded that the average Irish dairy farming was having a positive social impact for most stakeholders in 2013. Strong positive social impacts were found for value chain actors and society, predominantly positive impacts for local community and generally positive values for the worker. There were three very negative impacts: health and safety and equal opportunity/discrimination for workers and safe and healthy living conditions for the local community. It can be expected that these negative social hotspots might be ex- acerbated with expansion of the Irish dairy sector unless targeted policy is put in place to reduce their impact.

50: It can be concluded that the average Irish dairy farming was having a positive social impact for most stakeholders in 2013. Strong positive social impacts were found for value chain actors and society, predominantly positive impacts for local community and generally positive values for the worker. There were three very negative impacts: health and safety and equal opportunity/discrimination for workers and safe and healthy living conditions for the local community. It can be expected that these negative social hotspots might be ex- acerbated with expansion of the Irish dairy sector unless targeted policy is put in place to reduce their impact.

52: It can be concluded that the average Irish dairy farming was having a positive social impact for most stakeholders in 2013. Strong positive social impacts were found for value chain actors and society, predominantly positive impacts for local community and generally positive values for the worker. There were three very negative impacts: health and safety and equal opportunity/discrimination for workers and safe and healthy living conditions for the local community. It can be expected that these negative social hotspots might be ex- acerbated with expansion of the Irish dairy sector unless targeted policy is put in place to reduce their impact.

53: It can be concluded that the average Irish dairy farming was having a positive social impact for most stakeholders in 2013. Strong positive social impacts were found for value chain actors and society, predominantly positive impacts for local community and generally positive values for the worker. There were three very negative impacts: health and safety and equal opportunity/discrimination for workers and safe and healthy living conditions for the local community. It can be expected that these negative social hotspots might be ex- acerbated with expansion of the Irish dairy sector unless targeted policy is put in place to reduce their impact.

55: It can be concluded that the average Irish dairy farming was having a positive social impact for most stakeholders in 2013. Strong positive social impacts were found for value chain actors and society, predominantly positive impacts for local community and generally positive values for the worker. There were three very negative impacts: health and safety and equal opportunity/discrimination for workers and safe and healthy living conditions for the local community. It can be expected that these negative social hotspots might be ex- acerbated with expansion of the Irish dairy sector unless targeted policy is put in place to reduce their impact.

56: It can be concluded that the average Irish dairy farming was having a positive social impact for most stakeholders in 2013. Strong positive social impacts were found for value chain actors and society, predominantly positive impacts for local community and generally positive values for the worker. There were three very negative impacts: health and safety and equal opportunity/discrimination for workers and safe and healthy living conditions for the local community. It can be expected that these negative social hotspots might be ex- acerbated with expansion of the Irish dairy sector unless targeted policy is put in place to reduce their impact.

58: It can be concluded that the average Irish dairy farming was having a positive social impact for most stakeholders in 2013. Strong positive social impacts were found for value chain actors and society, predominantly positive impacts for local community and generally positive values for the worker. There were three very negative impacts: health and safety and equal opportunity/discrimination for workers and safe and healthy living conditions for the local community. It can be expected that these negative social hotspots might be ex- acerbated with expansion of the Irish dairy sector unless targeted policy is put in place to reduce their impact.

59: It can be concluded that the average Irish dairy farming was having a positive social impact for most stakeholders in 2013. Strong positive social impacts were found for value chain actors and society, predominantly positive impacts for local community and generally positive values for the worker. There were three very negative impacts: health and safety and equal opportunity/discrimination for workers and safe and healthy living conditions for the local community. It can be expected that these negative social hotspots might be ex- acerbated with expansion of the Irish dairy sector unless targeted policy is put in place to reduce their impact.

61: It can be concluded that the average Irish dairy farming was having a positive social impact for most stakeholders in 2013. Strong positive social impacts were found for value chain actors and society, predominantly positive impacts for local community and generally positive values for the worker. There were three very negative impacts: health and safety and equal opportunity/discrimination for workers and safe and healthy living conditions for the local community. It can be expected that these negative social hotspots might be ex- acerbated with expansion of the Irish dairy sector unless targeted policy is put in place to reduce their impact.

62: It can be concluded that the average Irish dairy farming was having a positive social impact for most stakeholders in 2013. Strong positive social impacts were found for value chain actors and society, predominantly positive impacts for local community and generally positive values for the worker. There were three very negative impacts: health and safety and equal opportunity/discrimination for workers and safe and healthy living conditions for the local community. It can be expected that these negative social hotspots might be ex- acerbated with expansion of the Irish dairy sector unless targeted policy is put in place to reduce their impact.
